video_title: Where the Smart Money Is Actually Going
gist: Dan Nathan and Guy Adami argue the AI/semiconductor trade has reached a blow-off top now hinging on high-bandwidth memory (Micron), citing a 10% overnight crash in South Korea's KOSPI and a 7% SOX drop off all-time highs as early signs of a global unwind. In the second half, Gutter Capital founders Dan Teran and James Gattinger lay out a contrarian VC thesis: skip the consensus 'metal-to-data-center-to-energy' infrastructure trade and the frontier-model labs, and instead invest cheaply at the application layer in overlooked industries.

themes.theme: Semiconductor blow-off top and global contagion

insights.claim: South Korea's KOSPI fell 10% overnight, a four-to-five standard deviation move, after roughly doubling in two months on heavy exposure to Samsung and SK Hynix (memory competitors to Micron), which Dan Nathan and Guy Adami read as a potential trigger for a worldwide unwind of the AI/semis trade.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: A 10% index-level move in a memory-levered market is a tail event worth treating as a contagion signal across the global AI complex, not a one-off.
insights.quote: When you see a 10% move, be it up or down, in this case down, in the index of a country, in this case South Korea, you have to take notice because obviously it's not something that happens every day.

insights.claim: The SOX semiconductor index made an all-time high on Monday and fell 7% the next day, with volatility bands widening dramatically into repeated 5-7% daily up-and-down swings, which Guy Adami interprets as emblematic of a market top rather than a bottom.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Widening volatility bands at all-time highs are a classic distribution/topping signature; investors should treat fresh highs in semis as exit liquidity rather than breakouts.
insights.quote: I think it's emblematic of a high given some of the levels we've reached in some of these individual names and these indices, specifically the semiconductors.

insights.claim: Dan Nathan argues memory, storage and pockets of semis are now the 'last bastion of hope' in the AI trade because software, internet stocks and the hyperscalers have all been selling off, implying investors should take chips off the table while leadership has narrowed.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Narrowing leadership down to a single sub-sector (memory) is a late-cycle tell; a break there leaves nothing holding up the AI complex.
insights.quote: If that is the last bastion of hope around the AI trade right here, I mean, it seems to be as if you're an investor, you got to take some chips off the table here.

insights.claim: Intel and Caterpillar have become 'meme stocks' trading on vibes and divorced from valuation, with Caterpillar up roughly 280% from its April 2025 post-trade-war lows while trading at ~40x earnings against ~29% sales growth.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: When industrial and chip names trade like meme stocks, valuation reverts hard on any demand disappointment; watch for the unwind in energy/data-center-adjacent industrials.
insights.quote: We've seen stocks like Intel become a meme stock. And Gene Munster called it that on Fast Money the other day... It's trading on vibes.

insights.claim: Energy-adjacent data-center beneficiaries including Caterpillar, GE Vernova and Bloom Energy all made all-time highs in the prior week, marking the AI-power trade as the latest area of frothy speculation.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Simultaneous all-time highs across the power-equipment basket flag crowding; these names carry the most downside if data-center build timelines slip.

themes.theme: Micron and high-bandwidth memory as the AI trade's pivot

insights.claim: Dan Nathan asserts the entire AI trade now hinges on Micron and high-bandwidth memory rather than Nvidia, a shift he says no one—including the memory makers themselves—had on their bingo card a year ago.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: If HBM/memory is the new fulcrum of the AI trade, Micron earnings become the single most important macro catalyst for the whole complex.
insights.quote: I did not think a year or 2 years ago that this entire AI trade hinges on Micron, because that's exactly what's going on right here... The story is high bandwidth memory.

insights.claim: Micron reports earnings after the close with the options market implying a ~10-11% move; the stock is up from a post-earnings low near $311 to roughly $1,100 (after an all-time high around $460 in March on the prior cohort of pricing), suggesting the bull case is already fully discounted.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: With a huge guide already in the price, a blowout quarter could still sell off; the asymmetry favors fading strength rather than chasing.
insights.quote: It traded as low, you ready for this, $311. It's trading right now at $1,100... So if you don't think that huge number and that huge guide is in the stock here at $1,100.

insights.claim: Micron's fundamentals have swung from a ~$4.50 loss on $15.5B sales in fiscal 2023 to expected earnings in the $60s per share on sales approaching $100B, which Nathan uses to argue the good news is already priced and should trade at a discount, not a premium.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Peak-earnings cyclicals deserve trough multiples; the dramatic fundamental improvement is the bear case once the cycle turns.
insights.quote: This year, they're expected to have literally 60-some dollars in earnings and sales that are just dwarfing what they had a few years ago. It's going to be like a hundred billion dollars.

insights.claim: Guy Adami contends the wrong time to buy commoditized cyclicals like Micron is when they screen 'dirt cheap,' and predicts the market will soon re-rate Micron back to being a highly cyclical, highly commoditized company—rejecting the secular-shift narrative.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: A framework for memory names: low trailing multiples at cycle peaks are value traps, not bargains—position for multiple compression, not expansion.
insights.quote: The wrong time to buy names like Micron is when they're dirt cheap, unless you think there's some sort of secular shift going on, which by the way, I don't think.

insights.claim: Nathan argues that because memory makers like Micron and SanDisk did not foresee the HBM demand cycle, they likely also lack visibility to execute on their backlog and remaining purchase obligations, undercutting confidence in forward guidance.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Poor demand-forecasting track records cast doubt on supply-side execution guidance—discount management's backlog confidence.
insights.quote: If they didn't have the visibility to model that out going forward, why would you suspect they have the visibility to model out their ability to execute on the backlog?

themes.theme: AI capex, rates and the forgotten consumer

insights.claim: A wave of capital raising—SpaceX's IPO at an $85B level plus an immediate $20B-scale debt raise, Google selling shares, and similar moves by Amazon and Meta—is funding an AI build estimated at $150-200B for a handful of names, pressuring the Nasdaq as hyperscaler stocks weaken.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Heavy equity and debt issuance to fund capex drains liquidity and weighs on long-duration tech; watch issuance pace as a top signal.

insights.claim: Nathan warns that AI-spend is a major driver of GDP growth, so if that capex moderates the economy reverts to leaning on the consumer (historically ~70% of spending) at a time when the low-end consumer is already weakened by inflation—a downside feedback loop the market is ignoring.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: AI capex is masking consumer weakness in GDP; a spending slowdown removes that cushion and exposes a fragile consumer—watch retailer commentary.
insights.quote: If GDP is being strongly affected, at least the growth, by the AI spend, at some point, if that were to moderate, then it goes back to a consumer to be 2/3 of the spend, and that consumer is going to be weakened.

insights.claim: Guy Adami says retailer and restaurant commentary describes a deteriorating consumer that is being masked by the AI narrative, arguing the market underweights the health of the ~70% of the economy that is the consumer.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Consumer-facing earnings calls are a cleaner read on real-economy health than AI headlines—use them as a leading recession signal.
insights.quote: If you listen to these retailers and a lot of the commentary from the restaurants... they tell you a much different story in terms of the health of the consumer.

insights.claim: Adami predicts the bond market will continue to deteriorate and that this market is 'not set up for higher rates,' which would compound technical damage on high-valuation long-duration tech now raising debt to fund AI capex.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Rising yields are a direct headwind to long-duration AI names raising debt; rates are the transmission mechanism for a tech de-rating.
insights.quote: My view that the bond market's going to continue to deteriorate, cuz we're not a market that's set up for higher rates.

insights.claim: Nathan flags that data-center construction is being pushed out even as capital is raised, warning that delayed builds imply coming overcapacity and that the jobs added by the build may be 'ephemeral.'
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Track build push-outs as the leading indicator of overcapacity; if construction jobs roll over, the labor-market support for the cycle reverses.
insights.quote: If you see push outs in the actual building of the data centers, then you have a real problem here because that suggests that there's going to be overcapacity.

insights.claim: Nathan likens the current setup to the dot-com bust and the financial crisis, noting Asian economies (South Korea, Taiwan) and their stock markets are heavily levered to AI spend while retail investors use margin—and warns US insulation via monopoly hyperscalers is overstated.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Margin-fueled, AI-levered Asian markets are the likely epicenter of a global unwind; US 'moats' won't fully insulate against a macro feedback loop.
insights.quote: This is what happened into the financial crisis. This is what happened in the dot-com. And don't think for a second here in the US that we are far more insulated.

insights.claim: SpaceX IPO'd around an $85B/$135 pricing level and traded down almost to that price (bouncing off ~$147) within days, which Adami cites as evidence of how quickly newly issued mega-cap supply can retrace to deal pricing.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Fresh mega-IPO supply retracing to its offer price within days signals weak demand for new paper—a warning for the issuance pipeline.
insights.quote: It is bouncing, but it's bouncing off of a 147 level that was basically mirroring the pricing level of 135. Think about that, how quickly we basically got down to where the IPO was priced.

insights.claim: Gutter Capital, founded by Dan Teran and James Gattinger, raised a $75M Fund III (after a $44M Fund II and a 2021 Fund I), running a concentrated portfolio with hands-on operational support and having done ~110 angel deals on their own balance sheet before taking outside capital.
insights.speaker: Dan Teran
insights.so_what: A small, operationally intensive seed fund deliberately scaling slowly is positioned to exploit the gap the mega-funds leave open.
insights.quote: Second fund was 44 million, third fund is 75 million... we invest in a concentrated portfolio, small group of companies, and we provide an exceptional level of hands-on support.

insights.claim: Gattinger says their angel track record, before fees and carry, consistently landed in the top 10% of funds year after year, which is what convinced him to formalize the partnership despite an unconventional background.
insights.speaker: James Gattinger
insights.so_what: A demonstrable top-decile angel record is the underwriting basis for the fund's operationally heavy thesis—evidence the model produces returns.
insights.quote: If you were applying fees and carry to what we were doing, we were consistently landing in the top 10% of funds year after year.

insights.claim: James Gattinger was a professional gambler for roughly a decade (online poker, then DFS on DraftKings/FanDuel with a software-driven edge), and applies that quantitative, profit-disciplined approach to venture investing alongside Teran's operator background.
insights.speaker: James Gattinger
insights.so_what: A quant/operator pairing is the differentiated edge the fund sells to LPs and founders—useful for assessing the team's underwriting discipline.
insights.quote: Prior to doing this, I was a professional gambler for about a decade where I played poker on the internet... and then fantasy sports on sites like DraftKings and FanDuel.

insights.claim: Teran's operating credibility comes from founding Managed by Q (sold to WeWork in 2019), where he later ran corporate development and a $200M creator fund and watched WeWork's failed IPO from the inside—experience he now uses to coach founders.
insights.speaker: Dan Teran
insights.so_what: First-hand exposure to a hyped IPO collapse informs the fund's skepticism of funny-money valuations and over-hyped rounds.

themes.theme: The 'most consensus market in venture history'

insights.claim: Teran calls this 'the most consensus market in the history of venture,' with about half of all venture money going to five firms and half of that going into rounds over half a billion dollars, leaving the other ~99% of founders facing the toughest pre-seed/seed market in history.
insights.speaker: Dan Teran
insights.so_what: Extreme capital concentration creates an arbitrage: under-served, non-consensus founders are mispriced and under-funded—where a small fund can find edge.
insights.quote: I think we're living through the most consensus market in the history of venture. You have like half the money going to five firms. You have half of that money going to rounds that are over half a billion dollars.

insights.claim: Median seed-round pricing has more than doubled to roughly $25-30M post-money while the number of deals is way down, creating a 'haves and have-nots' market where only ex-Apple/Stripe/Coinbase/Anthropic executives can easily raise.
insights.speaker: Dan Teran
insights.so_what: Bifurcated seed pricing means non-pedigree founders are starved of capital—an opening for funds willing to back scrappy, off-pattern teams cheaply.
insights.quote: It's either the haves and the have-nots. Either you are the VP from Apple, Stripe, Coinbase, you name it. You leave Anthropic, you get a big round.

insights.claim: Gattinger notes the median fund from the 2021 vintage is marked at less than 1x committed capital—worse than the Nasdaq—and argues LPs reacted to those losses by piling into risk-averse multi-billion-dollar mega-funds rather than learning that price and fundamentals matter.
insights.speaker: James Gattinger
insights.so_what: If the consensus mega-fund trade repeats the 2021 mistake of ignoring price, it sets up another sub-1x vintage—LPs should question the herd.
insights.quote: The median fund from that time is marked at less than 1X people's committed capital, which is way worse than the Nasdaq.

insights.claim: Gattinger argues mega-funds collecting ~2% annual fees on $10B checks have little incentive toward the long term, keeping companies private and pushing money into 'obvious winners' at any price because no one looks stupid chasing the trend.
insights.speaker: James Gattinger
insights.so_what: Fee-driven incentives, not return-maximization, explain mega-fund behavior—an agency-cost lens for spotting where capital is misallocated.
insights.quote: When you're clipping 2% annual fees on 10 billion dollar checks, you don't really have much of an incentive to the long term.

insights.claim: Teran launched Elbow Grease, a New York accelerator (now in its second cohort) to invest even earlier and take more risk on niche, non-consensus founders, citing 100+ applications within days and a community of ~15 companies and ~80 founders/operators in their Canal Street offices.
insights.speaker: Dan Teran
insights.so_what: A right-sized, earliest-stage vehicle is the structural answer to a market where standard seed product no longer fits founder needs.
insights.quote: The product that venture is offering the market today doesn't actually match with what founders need because founders are also able to do incredible amounts today with very little capital raised.

insights.claim: Gattinger argues Anthropic grew from ~$3B to a $45B run rate in a year—unprecedented in venture history—driven not just by having the best base model but by application-layer improvements (coding agent, Co-work) that made the models useful, while pure model quality is commoditizing.
insights.speaker: James Gattinger
insights.so_what: Value is migrating from base models to the application layer; the durable returns are in usefulness/distribution, not in owning the marginally-best model.
insights.quote: They've just grown from about 3 billion in revenue a year ago to 45 billion run rate... what has been expediting that growth has been the improvements they made at the application layer.

insights.claim: Gattinger contends spending billions for a 3-6 month model lead is a bad bet because the number-10 model is almost as good, the market is mispriced as if only two Western frontier labs will ever exist, and enterprising people will keep spinning out new labs—incompatible with the history of technology.
insights.speaker: James Gattinger
insights.so_what: Betting against frontier-lab consolidation: model leads are transient and fragmenting, so capital concentrated in two labs is likely to be over-rewarded today.
insights.quote: You pay multiple billions of dollars to have an advantage that lasts like 3 to 6 months if you're lucky... it is priced as if there will only ever be two frontier labs.

insights.claim: Gattinger points to model routing—sending simpler tasks to cheaper models because 'you don't need a Nobel laureate to summarize meeting notes'—as evidence demand will diversify across providers rather than consolidate to Anthropic.
insights.speaker: James Gattinger
insights.so_what: Routing structurally caps any single lab's pricing power and spreads spend across providers—a headwind to frontier-lab monopoly valuations.
insights.quote: You don't need like a Nobel laureate to be summarizing your meeting notes... you're also going to have demand being diversified to different model providers.

insights.claim: Teran says nearly every venture investor now repeats the same 'it starts at the metal, then the data center, then energy' thesis and chases the same deals, so Gutter deliberately looks elsewhere for value at the application layer.
insights.speaker: Dan Teran
insights.so_what: When every investor recites the identical infrastructure thesis, the trade is crowded and value has moved on—a signal to fade consensus.
insights.quote: How many podcasts have you listened to in the last two weeks where a different investor says, our thesis is it really starts at the metal and then you get to the data center and then you add energy.

insights.claim: Teran's core thesis is that you can build an excellent portfolio at the AI application layer—naming Rebuild (AI for restoration contractors), Faraday (HVAC back offices), Farm-to-table (AI drone software for forestry/agriculture), Recharged (used-EV marketplace)—without having any opinion on what Anthropic or long-term compute is worth.
insights.speaker: Dan Teran
insights.so_what: Application-layer bets in unglamorous verticals are compute-efficient with software margins and de-risk the valuation question on base models entirely.
insights.quote: You can have like an incredible portfolio of businesses and not have an opinion on what Anthropic is worth.

insights.claim: Teran frames the current moment as analogous to the post-internet infrastructure build, where the durable value accrued at the application layer in the 2000s, and argues AI is now proliferating into parts of the economy that incumbents won't adapt to fast enough.
insights.speaker: Dan Teran
insights.so_what: Using the internet analogy, the biggest long-run winners are application/vertical companies, not the infrastructure layer everyone is funding now.
insights.quote: It's not too different than what happened with the infrastructure build around the internet... it was the application layer ultimately in the 2000s where the billions turned out to be tens of billions and hundreds of billions and now is trillions.

themes.theme: Overvalued private markets and funny-money M&A

insights.claim: Nathan highlights SpaceX going public near a $2 trillion valuation while losing significant money, trading to a ~$3 trillion market cap, and then optioning to buy Cursor (a ~$60B price) for a company that raised at ~$10B in early 2025—calling SpaceX one of the most overvalued private companies ever now using public-market currency for expensive acquisitions.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Mega-cap newly public names using inflated stock as currency for overpriced M&A is a late-cycle hallmark; watch for value destruction in absorbed targets.
insights.quote: They had this option to buy Cursor, a company for $60 billion, that probably raised early 25 at $10 billion.

insights.claim: Nathan notes Cursor raised $900M at a $9.9B valuation a year ago and questions on what planet that value creation is justified, citing it as emblematic of an AI valuation disconnect that he believes will face an eventual reckoning.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Specific marker of private-market AI froth; a re-rating in names like Cursor would ripple through the cap tables holding them.
insights.quote: A year ago, Cursor raised $900 million at a 9.9 billion valuation. And like on what planet could you ever see that sort of value creation?

insights.claim: Teran observes that most venture funds' entry prices make a $3.6B outcome like Salesforce buying Intercom an 'unacceptable' result, so they ignore founders building at that scale—whereas Gutter structures itself to profit from $200M-to-$1B exits and avoid being adverse to founders taking a $3B exit.
insights.speaker: Dan Teran
insights.so_what: Entry price dictates the exit outcomes a fund can accept; lower entry prices unlock a large, ignored band of sub-billion M&A as attractive returns.
insights.quote: Most venture funds based on their entry prices look at that as an unacceptable outcome... that's just what's fundamentally different about how we operate.

insights.claim: Teran and Nathan warn that mega-IPOs with small floats can be gamed—release a small float, induce index funds to buy, then unlock the full VC cap table—creating misalignment and downside for index-fund buyers, with SpaceX and likely Anthropic/OpenAI fast-tracked into the Nasdaq.
insights.speaker: Dan Teran
insights.so_what: Small-float mega-IPOs plus forced index inclusion transfer risk to passive holders ahead of cap-table unlocks—a structural reason to be cautious on these names post-IPO.
insights.quote: It's easy to game. You can release the small amount of float and then induce the index funds to buy, and then you unlock the [cap table].

insights.claim: Nathan notes there are now more ETFs than stocks and that ~15 space-sector companies with $2-3B+ market caps were 'destroyed' over three trading days, illustrating how passive/issuance dynamics amplify both inflows into new mega-caps and the funding squeeze elsewhere.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: With ETFs outnumbering stocks, new mega-cap issuance like SpaceX drains capital from smaller sector peers—watch for forced rotation out of the 'space space.'
insights.quote: What's different now than like 20 years ago? There's more ETFs than there are stocks.

insights.claim: Teran predicts a wave of consolidation M&A in the $200M-to-$1B range as software businesses doubling annually at or near breakeven—dismissed by the venture market—still transact at 20-30x revenue, and argues the market these companies eventually sell into will differ from today's.
insights.speaker: Dan Teran
insights.so_what: Sub-billion software consolidation at 20-30x revenue is an under-discussed exit channel; profitable doublers are mispriced relative to where they can be sold.
insights.quote: The venture market decided that software businesses that were doubling every year at break even or profitably were no longer interesting. And we are happy to own those companies.
